quote_of_day.name: Anon

articles.title: Weather on the board today
articles.body:
A low-pressure system of theatre representation debates moved over the board, bringing scattered showers of personal anecdotes and photos. Anon shared experiences, with some posters defending diversity in casting while others expressed skepticism.

A weak front of professional envy brushed against the board, marked by complaints about a cousin's success. One anon expressed frustration about their cousin's high mileage and lucrative taxi business, feeling envious about their financial situation.

A cold snap of Olympic disappointment chilled the board, with anon lamenting India's poor performance. The criticism was direct, with some comparing India unfavorably to smaller countries with more Olympic medals.

A separate drizzle of celebrity mocking occurred, with one poster ridiculing a celebrity's reaction to a World Cup loss.

articles.title: Anon Debates Representation in Theatre, Shares Personal Photos
articles.body:
Anon sparked a debate about representation in theatre, specifically regarding the casting of black or Asian actors in traditional roles.

The original poster claimed that those who are upset about this trend have not watched enough theatre plays, citing the Royal Shakespeare Theatre and Greek Tragedies as examples.

Another anon countered that in some historical theatre productions, men played female roles, including in a YouTube video of "Julius Caesar" where Cassius is portrayed by a woman.

This led to a discussion about the history of theatre and the use of male actors in female roles.

The conversation quickly turned to skin tone and racial identity, with one anon claiming to be "whiter" than "aryan kangers" and another asking for proof of a person's racial identity.

Anon requested that another user post a photo of the back of their hand with a timestamp, which was met with resistance and jokes about skin tone.

Anon shared a photo of their wrist, claiming that it was representative of their skin tone. This led to more requests for photos and timestamps, with one anon eventually posting a response and asking another user to do the same.

The thread also included several personal attacks and insults, with one anon calling others "faggots" and "lemurs." Another anon claimed that Indian "steppe/aryan" kangers were examples of the "pajeet phenotype." The debate remained focused on issues of representation, racial identity, and personal authenticity, with many users questioning the claims and motives of others.

articles.title: Cousin's success sparks envy and frustration
articles.body:
Anon expressed feeling jealous and frustrated upon learning that their cousin, allegedly around 22-23 years old, is earning a net profit double their salary by driving a taxi.

The cousin's Toyota Innova Hycross Strong Hybrid is reportedly giving him 20 kmpl mileage.

Anon mentioned that they tried to console themselves by thinking they have room for growth, but then realized their cousin also has greater opportunities, including plans to buy a luxury bus and rent it out.

This led Anon to question how things have gone so far.

One respondent suggested that Anon's feelings might stem from a difference in social perception, with Anon being a white-collar worker and their cousin being a blue-collar taxi driver.

Another anon advised Anon to upskill and find a new job or start freelancing to improve their financial situation. Some anons shared their own experiences and observations.

One mentioned that they are 25 years old and are feeling angry and frustrated about their cousin's success at a younger age.

Another anon simply stated that they own a Toyota Crysta diesel BS4, which gives them 11 kmpl in the city and 17 kmpl on the highway.

The conversation also took a personal turn, with one anon making a comment about cross-dressing, which was met with a confused response from another anon. The thread also included a few off-topic comments and jokes.

articles.title: Anon Bemoans India's Olympic Performance
articles.body:
Anon expressed discontent with India's Olympic performance, stating that 'meme countries you have barely heard about have higher amount of olympic golds' and questioning how India could be 'this bad'.

Another anon responded by suggesting that Olympic gold medals are not as valuable as they seem, claiming that many gold medalists 'were abused as a child' and 'were subject to performance enhancing drugs.' This anon argued that the life outcomes of STEM professionals are generally better than those of Olympic athletes.

A different perspective was offered by an anon who noted that India's culture prioritizes studying over sports, which might explain the country's relatively poor Olympic performance.

This anon pointed out that many European and white countries place a high value on sports, whereas in India, it is not considered a prestigious pursuit.

One anon disputed the idea that Indians study to innovate, suggesting instead that they study to make money and are good at rote learning rather than grasping concepts.

Other responses included a claim that India actually won 70% of all gold medals in the last Olympics, which was likely false, and a sarcastic remark about redeeming the Olympics.

The discussion also touched on the topic of India's reputation as a 'vishwaguru' and the perceived value of Olympic medals as a measure of a country's success.

articles.title: Anon mocks celebrity over World Cup loss
articles.body: Anon posted 'Kekda. Monkey acts like monkey then wonder why he is called one'. Another anon replied that the celebrity 'does genuinely seem to like Ronaldo'. A respondent joked that the celebrity got 'trolled by Argentina fans'. One anon called the celebrity a 'subhuman' over a perceived loss, while another called them 'based'.
